A small teddy bear sits at the base of the stairs, a symbol of your earliest memories, and a forgotten pocket watch lies on the step, hinting at the passage of time. The staircase winds upwards, giving the impression of an infinite journey through one's own life, memories, and identity. The overall atmosphere is reflective, calm, and deeply personal, combining the architectural elegance of the spiral with the intimate theme of self-reflection.
themes: The central theme is Memory and Nostalgia. The photographs covering the walls show various snapshots of your life
creating a sense of nostalgia as you look back and reflect on your past experiences. Each photograph represents a piece of your personal history
giving the feeling that the staircase is a physical representation of memory itself
winding and continuous
leading you through different stages of life. The Passage of Time is a prominent theme
emphasized by both the presence of the pocket watch and the numerous photographs of different ages. The spiral staircase can also symbolize the cyclical nature of time
as well as the notion that we continuously revisit our memories
going back and forth through time as we reflect on our lives. The journey upward suggests progression

but also the weight of all the accumulated years. Another theme is Identity and Self-Reflection. The act of climbing the staircase while surrounded by photos of yourself at different stages of life reflects on how identity evolves over time. You
as the protagonist
are both the observer and the subject
creating a dual perspective of self—one that is living in the present and one that is remembering and contemplating the past. Childhood and Innocence are subtly present
represented by the teddy bear left behind on the step
a symbol of youth and innocence that has been outgrown. It suggests the inevitable separation from childhood
but also the emotional tether that remains
connecting the adult to their younger self. Loss and Forgetting are gently hinted at through the discarded pocket watch
symbolizing not only the passing of time but the things left behind or forgotten as we age. The watch may represent the fleeting nature of moments
and how
as we grow older
certain memories may fade or be left behind
even as others remain vivid. Lastly
the architectural grandeur of the spiral staircase evokes a sense of Journey and Continuity. The staircase seems endless
suggesting that this reflection on memory is not finite but an ongoing process
continuing as long as you continue to climb and reflect on your life.
images.teaser: What part of us keeps climbing when memory becomes both path and burden?

images.name: The Triple Monkey
images.description: The image titled "Triple Monkey" depicts a haunting post-apocalyptic scene drenched in the fiery hues of an ominous sunset. The foreground features a figure embodying a surreal, triple-faced gesture reminiscent of the "See No Evil, Hear No Evil, Speak No Evil" motif, symbolized by the hands obscuring different parts of the face. The surrounding environment is a desolate urban wasteland, with crumbling buildings, tangled power lines, and a debris-strewn road reflecting the intense golden light of the sunset. The dramatic sky, painted with swirling clouds and vivid orange tones, contrasts with the dark, desolate ground, amplifying the tension between destruction and beauty. The juxtaposition of the expressive figure and the devastated backdrop creates a sense of internal and external chaos.
themes: "Triple Monkey" explores themes of destruction
denial
and the human condition in the face of apocalyptic reality. The figure’s gestures allude to the refusal to confront harsh truths
symbolizing humanity’s tendency to ignore
avoid
or suppress awareness in moments of crisis. The fiery sky and ruined landscape suggest a catastrophic event
evoking themes of environmental degradation
war
or societal collapse. Simultaneously
the vibrant sunset imbues the scene with a paradoxical beauty
underscoring the complexity of finding aesthetic resonance in devastation. The central figure serves as both a witness and a participant in the collapse

reflecting on the shared culpability and existential struggle of individuals amidst collective ruin. This image provokes reflection on the tension between human denial and the inevitability of confronting reality.
images.teaser: What truths do we refuse when denial feels safer than witnessing collapse?

images.name: The Valley of Shadow of Death
images.description: In The Valley of Shadow of Death, you stand prominently, holding a lit lantern in the foreground. Your expression is serious, contemplative, and slightly anxious, suggesting an awareness of the weight of the journey ahead. The lantern emits a soft glow, representing the only visible source of light in the otherwise dark and ominous environment. Behind you stretches a deep, mysterious valley, shrouded in mist and flanked by towering, jagged mountains. The landscape feels forbidding, with dark shadows falling over its vast expanse, and several ghostly figures appear as small silhouettes in the distance, giving the impression of a journey through an underworld or spiritual plane. The scene evokes a sense of isolation, introspection, and facing a foreboding challenge.
themes: The most striking theme is Mortality
with the title directly referencing the famous phrase from Psalm 23
"the valley of the shadow of death." This suggests an exploration of mortality
the inevitability of death
and the personal confrontation with one's end. The image portrays a metaphysical journey
where you (the protagonist) must confront and navigate through the uncertainties and fears associated with life’s final chapter. Illumination in Darkness is another central theme
represented by the lantern you carry. The lantern signifies hope
knowledge
or guidance amidst the darkness of fear and uncertainty. The light could symbolize an inner strength or wisdom that helps one move forward
despite the ominous surroundings. The environment and composition also convey a strong sense of Solitude and Isolation. The vast
empty landscape and the distant figures create a feeling of being alone on this difficult path
reflecting the personal

often solitary nature of existential reflection and the journey toward self-understanding or spiritual awakening. Journey and Passage are symbolized by the valley and the path that stretches behind you. This journey is not just physical but also metaphorical
representing the passage through difficult life experiences
challenges
or the contemplation of the unknown. Lastly
there is a subtle theme of Fear of the Unknown or Existential Dread. The dark
misty valley
with its unclear destination and shadowy figures
suggests the fear and apprehension of what lies ahead
possibly reflecting anxieties about what happens after death or how one’s life will conclude.
images.teaser: What inner light do we trust when mortality walks beside us?
images.filename: The Valley of Shadow of Death.jpg

images.name: The Wall of Eternity
images.description: The image shows a vast, dark stone wall engraved with the names of well-known figures from history, culture, science, politics, and art. The surface is rough and scarred, as if repeatedly chiseled or eroded over time, and the engraved names—arranged in no obvious hierarchy—span the wall in dense rows. On the right side of the frame, a solitary man kneels on the floor, pressing his hands and forehead against the wall in a posture that suggests reverence, supplication, or exhaustion. At his feet lie simple tools: a hammer, a drill, and measuring tape, implying labor, construction, or inscription. The lighting is low and dramatic, emphasizing the texture of the stone and casting long shadows, while the man’s dark clothing causes him to visually merge with the wall, almost becoming part of it.
themes: The image reflects on legacy
memory
and the human desire to inscribe oneself into history. The engraved names evoke a collective canon of influence and remembrance—figures who shaped ideas
nations
and cultural consciousness—yet their sheer accumulation diminishes individual distinction
turning fame into an overwhelming mass. The kneeling figure introduces a personal dimension: a contemporary individual confronting the weight of inherited greatness
perhaps seeking belonging
recognition
or absolution in the presence of monumental predecessors. The tools on the floor suggest that entry into this wall of memory is not granted
but forged through effort
struggle
or even violence. At the same time
the act of pressing oneself against the wall implies futility: history is immovable
indifferent
and already crowded. The image thus meditates on ambition and humility
on the tension between the longing to be remembered and the sobering realization that even the most celebrated names eventually become just inscriptions in stone—silent
fixed
and unreachable.
images.teaser: What longing for permanence drives us to carve ourselves against oblivion?

images.name: The Well 1
images.description: This image strongly evokes themes of entrapment, introspection, and existential solitude. The well, a recurring symbol in literature and mythology, often represents the subconscious, a descent into one's inner world, or a state of forced contemplation. In the context of The Wind-Up Bird Chronicle, which inspired this piece, the well serves as a liminal space between reality and the unknown, a place where time dissolves, and the boundaries of self are tested. The protagonist in Murakami’s novel willingly enters the well in search of meaning, much like the figure here, who appears resigned to his descent, stripped of all but his thoughts. His nudity enhances the vulnerability of the moment, suggesting a confrontation with the self in its rawest form. The dimly lit, rough walls of the well reinforce the idea of time’s erosion, while the distant opening above presents an ambiguous contrast—does it offer the hope of escape, or is it merely a reminder of an unreachable world?
themes: The image depicts an elderly
nude man sitting at the bottom of a deep
cylindrical well
his skin mottled and textured like the eroded walls that surround him. The well extends upward
its rough
uneven opening revealing a distant patch of light
contrasting with the shadowy abyss below. The composition places the figure in the lower left corner
emphasizing his smallness in comparison to the vast
enclosing space. The dim
cold lighting casts a surreal
almost otherworldly atmosphere
accentuating the loneliness and entrapment of the scene. The overall color palette leans toward desaturated
bluish tones
reinforcing a sense of isolation and psychological depth.
images.teaser: What do we meet within ourselves when descent becomes deeper than escape?

images.name: The Well 2
images.description: This image explores themes of isolation, despair, and entrapment, both physical and existential. The well, a recurring motif in literature and mythology, signifies a descent into the subconscious—a space of forced self-examination and inescapable solitude. In relation to The Wind-Up Bird Chronicle, this visual interpretation mirrors the protagonist’s retreat into the well as a means of confronting hidden truths and inner turmoil. However, unlike Murakami’s character, who experiences surreal revelations in his descent, the figure in The Well_2 appears defeated, as though time has eroded all hope of escape or transformation. His posture suggests surrender rather than struggle, reinforcing the image’s existential weight. The well’s rigid brick structure, in contrast to the organic textures of the previous image, hints at a constructed prison—perhaps self-imposed, perhaps inevitable. The distant opening above remains an ambiguous presence: a reminder of an unreachable past, an indifferent world, or a final, distant glimmer of something beyond.
themes: The image presents an elderly man huddled at the bottom of a deep
cylindrical well. His posture is slumped
his head bowed
and his body wrapped in a tattered garment
emphasizing exhaustion and resignation. The walls of the well are structured in concentric
decayed brick layers
leading the eye toward the distant opening above
where a faint light filters in. The environment is dark
damp
and textured with organic and painterly strokes that blur the boundary between reality and psychological space. Shadows consume most of the well’s interior
further isolating the figure
while the rough textures and muted tones enhance the oppressive atmosphere.
images.teaser: How much of the self survives when isolation turns into resignation?

images.name: The Winds of Forgetting
images.description: This image explores the ephemerality of memory and the inescapable erosion of time. The hollowed head serves as a striking metaphor for cognitive decline, a symbol of aging minds losing their grasp on past experiences. The floating photographs represent memories—some vivid, some already slipping into oblivion—while the distant child stands as a reminder of an identity now out of reach. The barren desert setting reinforces the loneliness and inevitability of forgetting, where time and self slowly dissolve into the wind. The image evokes a sense of nostalgia, loss, and the fragile impermanence of human existence.
themes: In The Winds of Forgetting
an elderly man sits in an expansive desert
holding a fading photograph of his younger self. A metallic opening in his head reveals a deep void
from which fragments of memory disperse into the vast
hazy sky. Old photographs drift away
slipping from grasp
while in the distance
a ghostly young boy—perhaps the man’s past self—stands still
watching. The desolate landscape mirrors the emptiness of forgotten moments
where the past erodes like dust in the wind
leaving only whispers of what once was.
images.teaser: What remains of us when memory begins blowing out through the mind?

images.name: There's an Elephant in the Room
images.description: "There's an Elephant in the Room" humorously brings the idiom to life by placing an actual elephant within the elegant setting of a refined bedroom. The protagonist, seated on the elephant and guided by a mahout, appears nonchalant despite the absurdity of the situation. The elephant’s large presence, with its wrinkled skin and adorned with fabric, contrasts sharply against the clean, polished interior of the bedroom, creating a surreal, whimsical juxtaposition. The pristine bed and chandelier enhance the sense of displacement, where the enormous animal doesn’t belong in this intimate, enclosed space.
themes: This image plays on the idiom "the elephant in the room
" which refers to an obvious problem or issue that is being ignored or unaddressed. By literalizing this metaphor
the artist uses surrealism to explore themes of denial
avoidance

and discomfort in facing difficult truths. The calm demeanor of both the protagonist and the mahout suggests a willful ignorance or an acceptance of the absurdity. The absurdity and visual humor soften the underlying commentary on how easily people overlook significant issues
perhaps within personal or societal contexts. The elephant
traditionally a symbol of memory and wisdom
becomes a focal point of contradiction—its enormous
unavoidable presence clashes with the refined normalcy of the room
pushing the viewer to question what is left unspoken or unnoticed in their own lives.
images.teaser: Why do we ignore what feels too large and obvious to face?
images.filename: There's an Elephant in the Room.jpg

images.name: Things You Can See From Here
images.description: In the image titled "Things you can see from here," the protagonist stands in a bathroom, gazing at his reflection in a mirror while observing himself shaving. The scene is split between the real figure, fully dressed in a black shirt, and the reflection of the same figure in the mirror, shirtless, with shaving cream on his face. The physical space is a simple, clean bathroom, with soft lighting casting a warm glow on the beige tiles that surround the sink. The countertop is minimal, with a few toiletries placed neatly, including a can of shaving cream and other grooming essentials.
themes: One of the key themes in "Things you can see from here" is self-reflection and identity. The split between the protagonist in the physical world and his reflected self invites viewers to consider the multifaceted nature of personal identity. The protagonist’s act of watching himself in the mirror hints at introspection
where the man outside the mirror could be observing his past or future self. This moment of watching oneself perform a simple
everyday task becomes a deeper metaphor for contemplating one’s life
choices
and the person one has become. Another theme is the passage of time and routine. The act of shaving is deeply rooted in routine
something that must be done daily or regularly. In this context
the image captures the way life is composed of these repetitive actions

each day reflecting the one before it. The dressing of the outside figure and the undressed one inside the mirror may also speak to the dichotomy of the public self versus the private self—what we show to the world versus what we keep hidden. The bathroom mirror serves as a literal and figurative boundary between two realities or two versions of the same person. It introduces the theme of parallel selves—the idea that a person can exist in multiple states at once
influenced by time
memory
or even emotion. The image plays with this sense of parallelism
asking the viewer to consider what it means to look at oneself
not just physically but on a deeper

existential level. The title "Things you can see from here" suggests that the act of looking at oneself in the mirror reveals more than just a reflection. It points toward inner vision and understanding
as though the protagonist is seeing aspects of himself from a unique perspective. It hints at the idea that the mirror allows for a different kind of sight

one that penetrates deeper into the self or even beyond the present moment. The muted colors and soft lighting contribute to the theme of introspection and contemplation. The setting is familiar and mundane
but the act of reflection transforms the ordinary into a moment of existential inquiry
where the protagonist silently questions his own identity and the passage of time.
images.teaser: Which self is watching when reflection becomes more real than action?
images.filename: Things You Can See From Here.jpg

images.name: Thinking of thinking out of the box
images.description: "Thinking of Thinking out of the Box" features the protagonist sitting on a large cardboard box, while various geometric shapes — a pyramid, cylinder, cube, globe, and another cardboard box — float around him. The entire scene is set within a larger, plain cardboard box, creating a confined and somewhat minimalist environment. The expression on the protagonist's face suggests contemplation or perhaps confusion, as he holds a smaller cardboard box in front of him. The floating shapes imply abstract or intellectual concepts, and their arrangement within this confined space intensifies the focus on the protagonist’s mental engagement with these objects.
themes: This image explores the concept of "thinking outside the box" — a metaphor for creative
unconventional problem-solving. The scene challenges the literal and figurative meaning of the phrase

as the protagonist is not only seated within a box but is also seemingly bound by the geometric forms floating around him. The juxtaposition between his physical presence in the box and the abstract shapes highlights the tension between conformity and the desire for innovation. Themes of intellectual limitation
structure
and the boundaries of thought are evident

questioning whether true creativity can exist when one is trapped inside their own preconceptions. The image invites the viewer to reflect on their capacity to break free from mental constraints and embrace a more fluid
imaginative approach to problem-solving and life itself.
images.teaser: What confines us most when even imagination sits inside a box?
images.filename: Thinking of thinking out of the box.jpg

images.name: Three Monkeys
images.description: The image titled "Three Monkeys" presents a humorous yet thought-provoking exploration of themes such as silence, perception, and societal norms. Drawing inspiration from the classic "three wise monkeys" proverb—“see no evil, hear no evil, speak no evil”—this version offers a contemporary, and perhaps satirical, twist. Each figure is holding a banana, further adding an absurdist layer to the representation, which draws attention to the tension between serious cultural symbols and mundane, even comedic, elements.
themes: A key theme is conformity and absurdity
as all three versions of the protagonist are dressed identically and seated in the same position. This visual repetition hints at societal pressures for uniformity
while the masks
bananas
and formal setting contribute to the absurdity
challenging viewers to question the seriousness of these social expectations. Communication
or the lack thereof
is another major theme. With one figure wearing a mask over the mouth
another with a mask over the eyes
and the third with a mask covering the ears
the image directly references the proverb's caution about how we communicate—or fail to communicate—with the world around us. However
the use of face masks and bananas as symbols of protection or suppression humorously subverts this message
suggesting that in modern times
we may still be "blind" or "silent
" but often in arbitrary or nonsensical ways. Lastly
isolation and reflection are implied in the fact that the figures are all versions of the same individual
seated together yet disconnected in their awareness. The masks imply a barrier to full sensory engagement
even though they are physically close. This suggests a form of internal isolation or self-reflection
as if different parts of the self are in dialogue yet unable to fully connect. The pandemic context implied by the face masks adds a final layer to the piece. The masks
now a familiar object
evoke the idea of global experiences of isolation and restricted communication during the COVID-19 pandemic. Here
the masks serve not only as a barrier between individuals but also between oneself and full engagement with the world
further amplifying the sense of distance and disconnection
even when seated in the same room.
images.teaser: What do we preserve by refusing to see, hear, or speak?

images.name: As Time Flies By
images.description: In the image titled "Time is of the Essence," the protagonist is seated in a bright yellow chair, seen from behind, staring down an endless road that stretches toward the horizon. On either side of the road, large, uniformly spaced clocks line the path, creating a surreal and imposing atmosphere. The road itself is illuminated by warm, golden light, casting long shadows and drawing the viewer's eye toward the distant vanishing point. The clocks, each marking a different time, loom over the road like sentinels, their presence both omnipresent and foreboding.
themes: The primary theme in "Time is of the Essence" is the relentless passage of time and its inescapability. The endless road
lined with clocks
represents life’s journey
where time constantly surrounds and presses upon the individual. The protagonist
seated in a moment of stillness
faces the road ahead
seemingly contemplating the path that lies before him. The recurring motif of clocks
each showing different times
suggests that while time moves in one direction
its effects on the individual are multifaceted—there are memories of the past
worries about the future
and the fleeting present moment that slips away. The image evokes themes of aging
mortality
and the reflection on one’s life. The protagonist’s placement at the beginning of the road
facing the horizon
implies that time is something that stretches endlessly ahead
yet the clocks emphasize that it is also constantly ticking away. This dichotomy between the infinite and the finite creates a tension in the image
where the viewer is reminded of the inevitability of time’s march and the urgency of how one chooses to spend their moments. Another important theme is contemplation and acceptance. The protagonist
seated and calmly observing the road
may symbolize a meditative acceptance of time's passage. While the clocks might suggest the pressures of deadlines
responsibilities
and the ticking away of life
the protagonist’s stillness and the brightness of the chair indicate a moment of clarity or surrender to the flow of time. The road
bathed in warm
inviting light
suggests that this journey
though inevitable
might also be filled with moments of beauty and reflection. The birds in flight add a subtle layer to the theme of freedom versus constraint. While time
represented by the rigid
fixed clocks
imposes structure and limitation
the birds suggest a kind of release or transcendence beyond those limitations. This could indicate that while time governs much of our experience
there are moments or aspects of life that allow for freedom and release from its constraints. Overall
"Time is of the Essence" presents a vivid meditation on time’s power and the human experience of navigating its relentless flow. The protagonist’s quiet reflection within the surreal
time-dominated landscape invites the viewer to contemplate their own relationship with time
aging
and the path ahead.
images.teaser: How do we travel forward when time crowds every side of the road?

images.name: Tip of the Iceberg
images.description: In the image titled "Tip of the Iceberg," the protagonist's head is transformed into the form of an iceberg, floating in the vast, cold ocean. Only a small portion of the head, specifically the top of the head adorned with jagged, icy peaks, is visible above the waterline. The rest of the head, submerged underwater, reveals a much larger and more detailed form. Below the surface, the face of the protagonist is etched into the icy mass, showing a somber expression with a furrowed brow and lips pressed in contemplation or concern. The underwater portion is massive and heavily textured, dwarfing the visible part above water.
themes: The primary theme in "Tip of the Iceberg" is the duality between what is visible and what is hidden. The title references the well-known metaphor that what is seen above the surface is only a fraction of what lies beneath. This image suggests that the protagonist’s true self
emotions
or experiences are largely concealed below the surface
perhaps not easily accessible to others. The submerged
larger portion of the iceberg-like head represents the depths of the subconscious
unresolved emotions
or hidden facets of identity that are rarely revealed to the outside world. The underwater portion of the face shows a more introspective and somber expression
possibly indicating that the deeper aspects of the protagonist’s inner self are weighed down by concerns
thoughts
or emotions that remain unspoken or unseen. The visible portion
with jagged icy peaks
could symbolize the more external
hardened persona that the protagonist presents to the world—cold
distant
or impenetrable. The presence of the shark introduces a theme of danger or threat. The shark could symbolize external forces that lurk just beneath the surface
potentially threatening the stability of the protagonist’s submerged identity or internal life. This predator-like figure adds tension to the scene

suggesting that what lies beneath the surface is not only larger and deeper but also fraught with risk or peril. The shark also symbolizes the unknown dangers that often accompany deep introspection or self-exploration
where diving into hidden emotions or thoughts may uncover uncomfortable truths. The waterline serves as a metaphor for the boundary between the conscious and subconscious mind. Above the water
only the "tip" of the iceberg is visible
representing the limited view others have of the protagonist’s identity
thoughts
or emotions. Below the waterline lies the vast

unexplored territory that constitutes the majority of the self. This theme of concealment and introspection resonates with the idea that much of human experience and personal identity is hidden from view
with only fragments visible to others. The cold
remote environment emphasizes themes of isolation and detachment
suggesting that the protagonist’s hidden self is in some way cut off from the external world. This setting reinforces the sense of internal reflection
where the protagonist is both physically and emotionally distant from others
surrounded by the icy waters of solitude and introspection.
images.teaser: What hidden depths shape us long before they rise into view?

images.name: Trapped in Stone
images.description: The image, titled "Trapped in Stone," presents a striking visual of a human face embedded within a weathered block of stone. The face, partially obscured and cracked, appears to be an organic part of the stone, blending seamlessly with its rough texture. Vines and roots weave through the cracks, further entwining the human form with the natural decay of the stone. The surface is marked with faint discolorations, hinting at the passage of time and erosion. The muted tones of the composition, combined with the dark background, create a somber and introspective atmosphere, emphasizing the feeling of entrapment and timelessness.
themes: "Trapped in Stone" explores themes of entrapment
transformation
and the interplay between humanity and nature. The embedded face symbolizes a loss of identity or individuality
as it becomes inseparable from the inanimate stone. The vines and roots suggest both decay and resilience
representing nature's reclamation of all things
including human creations and existence. The image evokes a sense of timelessness
as though the figure has been trapped for centuries
silently enduring the erosion of both body and spirit. Thematically
it delves into the inevitability of change and decay
highlighting the fragility of human life in the face of nature’s permanence. It invites viewers to reflect on their own legacies and the ways in which they might be shaped—or consumed—by time.
images.teaser: What part of us hardens when feeling can no longer stay alive?

images.name: Trapped in Time
images.description: The image, titled "Trapped in Time," depicts a haunting vision of a human face partially embedded within a fractured and weathered block of stone. The face, with closed eyes, exudes a sense of stillness and introspection, as if frozen in a moment of eternity. Metallic elements, such as a curved structure resembling a clock hand or gear, intertwine with the organic cracks and vines that snake across the surface. The textured stone carries marks of age, erosion, and decay, complemented by the surrounding earthy tones and subtle light. The juxtaposition of organic and mechanical details highlights the merging of natural and temporal forces.
themes: "Trapped in Time" explores themes of mortality
the passage of time
and the enduring interplay between humanity and the forces of nature. The embedded face
with its serene expression
suggests resignation or acceptance of the inevitable constraints of time. The metallic elements evoke the mechanisms of a clock
symbolizing the rigid
unyielding march of time
while the vines and cracks hint at nature’s slow but relentless reclamation of all things. The closed eyes emphasize introspection and the transient nature of human existence
as though the figure is lost in reflection
caught between permanence and decay. The image invites viewers to contemplate the fragility of life and the inexorable progression of time
evoking a profound sense of existential stillness.
images.teaser: What quiet surrender appears when time itself turns us into relics?

images.name: TRex Nightmare
images.description: The image, titled "T-Rex Nightmare," portrays a surreal and intense scene of terror unfolding in a dimly lit bedroom. An elderly man lies asleep on a bed, clutching a pillow with a peaceful, vulnerable expression. Above him, the wall explodes violently, as the enormous head of a roaring T-Rex bursts through, its gaping jaws and sharp teeth capturing the moment of primal chaos. Debris and fragments of the shattered wall scatter across the frame, adding motion and tension. A broken picture frame lies on the bed, reflecting an earlier, distorted glimpse of the destruction. The contrasting tranquility of the sleeping figure and the erupting violence of the dinosaur create a striking juxtaposition, blending dreams and nightmares in a single frame.
themes: "T-Rex Nightmare" explores themes of vulnerability
fear

and the boundary between reality and imagination. The explosive entry of the T-Rex symbolizes the sudden intrusion of overwhelming fears or unresolved anxieties into the subconscious. The elderly man’s serene demeanor amidst the chaos underscores the fragility of human existence
especially in the face of overpowering and uncontrollable forces. The shattered wall and broken picture frame suggest the destruction of safety and order
a common motif in nightmares where the familiar becomes threatening. The image captures the tension between the primal and the personal
inviting viewers to reflect on their own fears and how they manifest in moments of perceived safety. It is a visceral depiction of how internalized fears can erupt into vivid
unsettling dreams.
images.teaser: Which buried fear crashes through sleep when the mind can no longer contain it?

images.name: Up the wall
images.description: The image titled "Up the Wall" vividly represents the metaphorical expression "it drives me up the wall." In this surreal scene, the protagonist, yourself, is seen literally suspended horizontally against the wall, seemingly defying gravity. The room is dimly lit, with worn textures on the walls, evoking a sense of discomfort or tension. Below, a vintage TV set on a dresser reflects the same scene on its screen, enhancing the absurdity and reiterating the frustration depicted. The leather chair in the room stands empty, symbolizing an inability to sit still or relax. On the wall, a portrait of a young girl adds an element of nostalgia or contrasting calmness in the midst of the turmoil. The image emphasizes the tension of being "driven up the wall," visually portraying the stress, frustration, or agitation associated with the phrase.
themes: The themes in this piece delve into self-perception and the distortion of reality. The mirrored image on the TV
a constant within your work
hints at introspection or an exploration of alternate realities
where the self is observed through different lenses. The defiance of gravity and the odd suspension suggest a feeling of detachment or perhaps a loss of control
floating without direction. The neglected setting contrasts with the vibrant yet innocent portrait of a child
potentially symbolizing a distant past or forgotten innocence. The composition explores the disorientation that comes with aging or the exploration of one's identity in unfamiliar
unyielding spaces. The piece speaks to the idea of being overwhelmed by inner narratives
visually distorted through the surreal framing.
images.teaser: What pushes us upward when reality itself begins to feel unhinged?

images.name: Hostage 1
images.description: A monochrome image depicts a solitary figure wrapped tightly in a white cloth that conceals the head and upper torso, transforming the body into a faceless, sculptural form. The figure leans forward, gripping a rope that extends beyond the frame, while the wrists appear bound, merging restraint and exertion into a single gesture. The contrast between the smooth, draped fabric and the rough texture of the rope and exposed arm emphasizes physical tension. The background is stark and unadorned, isolating the figure and focusing attention on posture, strain, and the direction of the unseen force pulling against them.
themes: The image explores the paradox of control and submission
where resistance and captivity coexist within the same act. The bound figure is neither passive nor fully autonomous; the tension in the body suggests effort
endurance
and a refusal to collapse
even as freedom is visibly restricted. By concealing the face
the work removes individuality and narrative specificity
allowing the figure to function as a universal symbol of psychological
social
or existential constraint. The rope becomes both a literal and metaphorical line of force
representing obligations
power structures
or internal compulsions that pull the individual forward. The image ultimately reflects on the human condition as one of persistent negotiation between agency and limitation
dignity and restraint.
images.teaser: What freedom remains when control and captivity occupy the same body?

images.name: Volcanic Eruption
images.description: The image, titled "Volcanic Eruption," vividly merges the human form with the raw power of a volcanic landscape. The profile of a human face, composed of cracked volcanic rock, glows with fiery veins of molten lava coursing through its fissures. Smoke and ash billow from the top of the head, mirroring the eruption of an active volcano. The fiery elements illuminate parts of the face, particularly the eyes, which appear reflective and contemplative amidst the chaos. The surrounding atmosphere, filled with dramatic clouds of ash and smoke, creates a sense of both destruction and awe. The interplay of textures—rugged rock, fiery lava, and swirling smoke—brings the composition to life, evoking a powerful and dynamic transformation.
themes: "Volcanic Eruption" explores themes of inner turmoil
transformation
and the raw force of human emotion. The volcanic imagery symbolizes the intensity of suppressed emotions breaking free
paralleling a physical eruption. The face
with its glowing fissures
suggests the duality of strength and vulnerability—human resilience juxtaposed with fragility. The eruption represents not just destruction but also renewal
as volcanic activity creates fertile ground for new growth. This imagery may also reflect the connection between humanity and nature
suggesting that the forces within us mirror the primal energies of the Earth. The image invites viewers to consider their own inner landscapes
where hidden pressures and emotions might one day erupt
reshaping their lives and perspectives.
images.teaser: What erupts when buried emotion can no longer remain sealed inside?

images.name: Stepping on Eggshells II
images.description: "Walking on Eggshells" offers a striking visual metaphor where the protagonist, using walking poles, balances precariously on large, fragile eggs. One egg has already cracked, its golden yolk spilling out, further emphasizing the delicacy of the situation. The mountainous background adds a sense of journey or challenge, as if the figure is navigating a perilous and uncertain path. The oversized eggs dwarf the protagonist, suggesting the fragility of his environment and the potential for things to fall apart with a single misstep.
themes: The image plays directly on the idiom "walking on eggshells
" which refers to the feeling of needing to be extremely cautious in a sensitive situation. The scene underscores vulnerability
both physically and emotionally
as the protagonist treads carefully on a surface that could break at any moment. Themes of fragility
balance
and tension permeate the image
symbolizing the delicate nature of human relationships or circumstances where the wrong move could cause irreversible damage. The cracked egg and spilled yolk hint at past mistakes or consequences
while the journey over such a treacherous terrain suggests resilience and the effort to continue despite the risk. There's an exploration of aging and life's fragility
with the protagonist embodying perseverance in a world that demands careful navigation.
images.teaser: How much of ourselves do we suppress to avoid cracking fragile peace?

images.name: Welcome to the Mirror World
images.description: In Welcome to the Mirror World, you, the protagonist, are standing in a dimly lit room, gazing toward an ornate mirror that reflects a deeper reality. The mirror portrays an extension of yourself reaching out to pull back a curtain, suggesting the existence of a hidden or parallel world beyond its surface. The room is bathed in cool, greenish-blue tones, with a solitary, eerie rocking horse sitting still on the floor, its shadow cast beneath it. On the wall, a framed painting of a woman, shrouded in green light, adds to the sense of mystery. The lighting is dramatic, creating deep shadows that enhance the surreal and contemplative atmosphere. The perspective pulls the viewer into the mirrored space, as if inviting them to explore what lies beyond.
themes: This image strongly evokes themes of duality and alternate realities. The mirror is a portal
symbolizing the passage between the known and the unknown
the self and the other. The figure reflected in the mirror is an extension of you
suggesting an exploration of the hidden aspects of self
perhaps indicating introspection or a desire to uncover deeper truths about your identity. Another theme is nostalgia or a reflection on childhood. The rocking horse
an object associated with innocence and youth
contrasts with the dark and ominous tone of the room. Its presence adds a layer of melancholy or a reminder of the passage of time
linking it to ideas of memory
lost innocence
or the inaccessibility of the past. The painting of the woman on the wall may introduce themes of memory or longing. She could represent a distant figure from the past
someone who exists in the periphery of your consciousness but remains unreachable
adding to the sense of a world that is both familiar and unreachable. Lastly
the image’s surrealistic qualities touch on the boundaries between reality and imagination. By presenting an invitation to step into the mirror world
the work questions the nature of perception and reality
prompting the viewer to consider what lies behind the surface of everyday life.
images.teaser: What part of us crosses over when reflection becomes a doorway rather than image?

images.name: Whispers of Eternity
images.description: The image portrays a surreal scene at sunset, blending human fragility with the immensity of nature. In the foreground, a weathered human face, resembling a statue or sculpted head, emerges partially submerged in water, with its features worn and cracked, symbolizing the passage of time. Dynamic splashes of water frame the head, creating a sense of motion and tension against the serene backdrop. The distant landscape features ancient ruins, resembling classical Greek columns, positioned on an isolated plateau. The golden and azure hues of the sky contrast with the turbulent water and evoke a dreamlike, eternal quality.
themes: This composition explores themes of transience
eternity
and the cyclical nature of existence. The weathered face suggests the impermanence of human life and the erosion of identity over time
while the ruins allude to ancient civilizations and their enduring legacy amidst inevitable decay. The water
a symbol of both life and relentless change
connects the ephemeral human condition with the continuity of nature. The juxtaposition of the serene sky and chaotic water emphasizes the tension between permanence and fragility
inviting contemplation of our place within the vast timeline of history and existence.
images.teaser: What do we hear within ourselves when ruin begins speaking the language of time?

images.name: Auto Portrait with a Pendulum Clock
images.description: In an opulent library drenched in baroque elegance, a man's head appears peeled open like an orange, each layer revealing not flesh or bone but clock faces and intricate gears. His solemn, timeworn expression persists through the mechanical strata, eyes peering out from a slice that straddles humanity and machinery. The golden Roman numerals and brass mechanisms replace the inner anatomy, suggesting a mind entirely constructed—or consumed—by the passage of time. The surreal dissection blurs the boundaries between man and timepiece, biography and mechanism.
themes: The image explores the existential entanglement of identity and temporality. By visualizing the human head as a mechanical clock
the image evokes the dehumanizing precision and inevitability of time
portraying aging not as a gradual process but as a peeling away of humanity to expose the machinery beneath. The lavish setting—a classical library—contrasts with the disassembled portrait
hinting at a life of thought
memory
and culture now being dissected by time’s relentless gears.
images.teaser: Who are we when time becomes the mechanism beneath thought itself?
images.filename: Auto Portrait with a Pendulum Clock.jpg

images.name: Inner Demons
images.description: An elderly man sits in near darkness, illuminated only by the greenish glow of his monitor, where he edits a surreal image of his own head peeled open like an orange. Around him, like projections from his psyche, distorted versions of his own face scream in agony and rage, their mouths wide open in silent accusations. These apparitions do not threaten from without—they echo from within. "You’re not good enough." "This is worthless." "You’re an impostor." They are the voices that haunt the creative mind, especially in solitude, surfacing in the quiet hours when self-doubt speaks loudest.
themes: Inner Demons is a chilling visual meditation on the emotional cost of creation. It lays bare the psychological warfare waged during the act of self-expression
particularly in late adulthood. The peeled orange head symbolizes exposure
vulnerability
and the obsessive scrutiny artists often inflict upon themselves. The screaming figures on the wall are manifestations of the inner critic—harsh
persistent
and inescapable. Their taunts—rooted in fear of inadequacy and impostor syndrome—are not just imagined but felt. This image grapples with identity
self-worth
the burdens of perfectionism
and the invisible battles behind every artistic gesture.
images.teaser: What inner enemies awaken when creation demands more than the self can bear?

images.name: Photographing Leo
images.description: In a dimly lit Renaissance studio, Leonardo da Vinci sits before an easel, meticulously applying brushstrokes to what will become his most famous masterpiece: the Mona Lisa. However, the model posing for the painting—draped in the familiar dark dress and seated in the iconic pose—lets out an unmistakable yawn, her mouth open mid-expression, betraying a very human impatience. This unexpected detail injects humor and realism into an otherwise reverent scene. To the right, a modern elderly man with a digital camera—clearly from another century—leans in to capture the moment, his presence anachronistic yet oddly natural within this surreal composition. The setting is warm and atmospheric, with a large-paned window diffusing soft daylight across a wooden table scattered with brushes, vessels, and an open notebook—further grounding the image in the quiet hum of creative labor, just delightfully interrupted.
themes: This image playfully unravels the mythology of genius and timeless art. By catching the Mona Lisa mid-yawn
the composition punctures the illusion of divine inspiration with a touch of everyday fatigue
reminding us that even the most iconic moments may have been laced with boredom
discomfort
or distraction. The presence of a contemporary photographer—calmly documenting the scene with a digital camera—serves as a self-aware commentary on the modern urge to witness
capture
and reinterpret history. The image blurs not only the boundaries of time but also of authorship: who owns the moment
the painter or the observer? It gently mocks our reverence for "perfection" in art
suggesting that behind every masterpiece lies a complex reality—less divine
more human.
images.teaser: What happens to genius when fatigue and playfulness enter the sacred scene?

images.name: Kitty Hawk December 1903
images.description: In a sepia-toned landscape of windswept dunes and soft skies, the historic Wright Flyer soars just above ground, faithfully rendered with its forward canard wings, rear propellers, and prone pilot. Beneath it—caught mid-duck—is a modern-day time-traveling photographer, clearly overwhelmed. He clutches a Delta-branded travel bag and a DSLR camera while shielding his head, as his baseball cap lifts into the air behind him. The flat sands, gentle slope, and blurred propeller motion root the moment in Kitty Hawk on December 17, 1903—but the figure’s attire and posture rupture the illusion. It’s a clean, cinematic collision between mythic aviation history and contemporary misadventure.
themes: This image speaks to the disorientation of encountering a pivotal moment in human progress not as a hero or participant—but as an accidental tourist. The photographer’s cowering posture juxtaposed with the soaring Flyer undercuts the typical triumphal narrative of invention with humor and humility. It raises questions about how we view the past: as a series of framed milestones
or as chaotic
unstable realities. The presence of modern luggage and branding amid the sacred sands of Kitty Hawk pokes fun at how we commercialize legacy and mythologize human achievement. Above all
the image wryly reminds us that history often happens barely above our heads—and we’re rarely as prepared for it as we imagine.
images.teaser: What awakens in us when progress arrives while fear still clings to the ground?

images.name: The Gentle Hands of the Executionaire
images.description: Set against the brooding atmosphere of Revolutionary Paris, the image captures the charged moment before a public execution at the guillotine. A regal woman, likely a queen, stands with dignified composure beside the execution platform, while a modern-day time-traveling photographer is gripped firmly by the executioner. His wide eyes and tense posture suggest not amusement, but fear and confusion—staring helplessly at a history he didn’t intend to enter. With a DSLR camera and passport in hand, clad in present-day clothing, he is absurdly misplaced in a scene drenched in solemnity. The painterly sepia palette and dense, turbulent crowd heighten the tension between eras, emotion, and intent.
themes: This image explores the absurdity of historical dislocation
highlighting how profoundly out of place—and powerless—a modern observer becomes when stripped of context and control. The time-traveling photographer
armed with tools of documentation but no agency
finds himself at the mercy of a world that predates his values
his technology
and his sense of security. His frightened expression and awkward presence underscore the tension between past brutality and present-day detachment. The guillotine
once a symbol of revolutionary justice
looms not just as a historical object but as a reminder that history is not a sanitized museum—it was lived
suffered
and feared. The piece humorously critiques our instinct to witness rather than act
and to photograph rather than understand.
images.teaser: What remains of dignity when history turns us into powerless witnesses?
images.filename: The Gentle Hands of the Executionaire.jpg

images.name: What holds you now
images.description: In What Holds You Now?, an elderly man sits hunched in the cracked palm of an enormous marble hand that is in the midst of disintegration. Fragments burst outward from the hand’s structure, frozen mid-collapse, while the man remains motionless — a figure of silence and surrender amid chaos. The neutral gradient in the background suggests a transition from darkness to light, further emphasizing the contrast between stability and collapse, weight and weightlessness. This monochrome self-portrait blends realism with symbolism, inviting viewers into a suspended moment of existential stillness.
themes: This image explores the fragility of support systems — physical
emotional
or temporal — and the inevitable erosion of what once seemed solid. The crumbling hand
once a symbol of shelter or time itself
now becomes the very site of collapse. The figure
seemingly unaware or resigned
confronts the unsettling question: when the structures we lean on disintegrate
what remains to hold us? What Holds You Now? addresses the themes of aging
mortality
and identity dissolution with a quiet intensity
offering a meditation on impermanence and the solitary nature of the human condition.
images.teaser: What supports us when the very hand holding us begins to crumble?

images.name: The Cubist Time Traveler
images.description: Set in Pablo Picasso’s sunlit studio, this image captures an impossible encounter: a modern-day man—clearly out of place—sits as the master paints his Cubist portrait. The same man appears again in the corner, camera in hand, documenting the moment as it unfolds. Yet the camera’s viewfinder betrays a paradox—the image displayed does not reflect the real scene. Instead, it shows a distorted version of the portrait-in-progress, misaligned with the physical reality it observes. This layering of presence, perception, and artistic interpretation blurs the boundaries between subject, observer, and illusion.
themes: The Cubist Time Traveler investigates the fragmentation of identity through time
art
and memory. By placing the same figure as both sitter and witness
the work literalizes the Cubist principle of multiple perspectives—but with a temporal twist. The camera
expected to capture truth
instead reveals a distorted or alternate version of events
echoing the painting’s own refusal to mirror reality. This deliberate mismatch challenges the reliability of both artistic and technological representation. Through irony and visual contradiction
the image raises timeless questions: What is truth in art? And who really owns the image of the self—its creator
its subject
or its observer?
images.teaser: Who are we when art fractures us into witness, subject, and memory?

images.name: Ceci nest pas toi
images.description: In a surreal homage to René Magritte, an elegantly dressed man stands before a gilded mirror, his face obscured by a vibrant green apple. Outside the reflection, another figure—identical in posture but holding a camera—photographs the mirror, his face hidden by a red apple. Behind a curtain, Magritte himself peeks out, pipe in hand, a quiet witness to the absurdity. At the bottom of the frame, inscribed in delicate script, is the phrase "Ceci n’est pas toi"—"This is not you."
themes: This image challenges the viewer’s assumptions about identity
perception
and the nature of representation. By invoking Magritte’s iconic motifs—the apple
the bowler hat

the contradiction between text and image—it blurs the boundary between observer and subject. The reflection does not reflect; the photographer does not see. The presence of both a red and green apple underscores duality: the self we show versus the self we perceive. The phrase "Ceci n’est pas toi" adds a layer of irony
suggesting that even in visual form
authenticity is elusive.
images.teaser: What part of us disappears when image, reflection, and self refuse to align?

images.name: The Persistence of Melting
images.description: The Persistence of Melting” is a surreal self-portrait in which the artist, cast as a time-traveling photographer, crouches behind a barren tree attempting to document a reality that is dissolving before his eyes. A young girl in red holds a golden watch like a sacred relic, while Salvador Dalí paints the moment—unaware that he himself is part of the composition. Melting clocks drape over stone, wood, and air, echoing time’s decay, and the photographer’s camera lens droops, as if history itself is too unstable to capture. The image unfolds in golden light, dreamlike and delicate, suspended between absurdity and reverence.
themes: This work reflects the artist’s ongoing exploration of time
identity
and perception. By placing himself within a Dalí-inspired world
he questions the very act of artistic observation: Can we document memory before it melts away? The melting clocks symbolize the impermanence of experience
while the presence of Dalí and the child suggest a dialogue between imagination and innocence. The time-traveling self becomes a paradox—both witness and participant
outsider and creator. “The Persistence of Melting” is not only a tribute to surrealism
but a meditation on the fragile act of remembering.
images.teaser: Can we hold reality together when time itself begins to melt away?

images.name: Lakehurst, May 1937
images.description: In The Last Frame – Lakehurst, 1937, Dov Fuchs places himself twice within one of the most iconic disasters of the 20th century. As the Hindenburg bursts into flames above the Lakehurst Naval Air Station, one version of the artist flees in terror while another calmly photographs the unfolding catastrophe. Rendered in rich sepia tones and period attire, the image blends historical reconstruction with surreal self-portraiture, evoking the visual language of early press photography while subverting it through playful duplication and anachronism.
themes: This work explores the absurd tension between documentation and experience
between presence and survival. By duplicating himself as both witness and victim
Fuchs highlights the paradox of the artist who observes even while in peril. The image blurs the boundary between personal narrative and collective memory
raising questions about identity
detachment
and the role of the storyteller in historical tragedy. With irony
elegance
and wit
The Last Frame becomes a visual metaphor for our need to make sense of disaster — even as it consumes us.
images.teaser: What do we choose to see when disaster erupts around our need to witness?

images.name: The Last Frame, North Atlantic, April 1912
images.description: The Last Frame places the artist at the heart of the Titanic disaster, fractured into multiple selves: one drowning in the icy water, another calmly photographing the scene from a lifeboat. A third figure — a woman in a life vest — screams in panic beside him. With surreal clarity and vintage aesthetics, the image reimagines this iconic tragedy as a moment of quiet absurdity, where presence, detachment, and self-duplication turn catastrophe into a darkly intimate tableau.
themes: This work interrogates the act of witnessing — and what is lost in the process. By inserting himself as both victim and observer
the artist explores the tension between engagement and disconnection
between survival and documentation. The presence of a panicked stranger contrasts sharply with the indifference of the photographer
exposing how memory
trauma
and identity fracture under historical weight. As with other images in the Historic Moments series
The Last Frame blurs the boundaries between personal myth and collective memory
between what happened and what is seen.
images.teaser: What is lost within us when survival and observation become the same act?
images.filename: The Last Frame, North Atlantic, April 1912.jpg

images.name: Error 404 Gravity Not Found
images.description: A sepia-hued study dissolves everyday reality: a middle-aged man, shirt half-untucked and tie fluttering, hovers diagonally across a modest room while papers, a lone shoe, a mustard-yellow chair, and an open laptop orbit him in slow motion. On the laptop’s glowing screen, the entire scene repeats in an infinite digital loop, turning the workspace into a recursive dream. Subtle shadows pin the floating objects to believable space, yet the overall atmosphere feels weightless—an office snapshot caught in the instant gravity called in sick.
themes: “Error 404: Gravity Not Found” transforms a mundane work setting into a witty allegory for modern disconnection. By replacing the familiar web glitch with a physical one
the piece riffs on how digital life can upend our sense of grounding: files scatter like thoughts
a chair loses its purpose
and even time itself (suggested by the looping screen) becomes a self-referential echo. The absent shoe and loosened tie hint at an identity slipping its formal constraints
while the sepia palette folds nostalgia into the present glitch
asking whether the comforts of routine can survive in an era where reality is always one bug—or one browser tab—away from floating off.
images.teaser: What breaks first when modern life strips us of all inner gravity?

images.name: The Haute Couture Tailors of Delusions
images.description: In The Haute Couture Tailors of Delusion, two elaborately dressed tailors — both portrayed by the artist — perform their solemn duties around a regal, bare-chested king who stands crowned in an opulent chamber. One takes careful measurements while the other advises with theatrical flair. Behind the king, a gilded mirror reveals his unclothed back and a pair of golden scissors floating mid-air — a silent testament to the illusion of a robe that exists only in imagination. The contrast between the rich, ceremonial atmosphere and the invisible garment lies at the heart of this satirical composition.
themes: This image reimagines Andersen’s fable as a deeply personal
psychological allegory. The artist
split into three roles — king and two tailors — stages a drama of illusion
ego
and complicity. The tailors act with gravity and skill
despite having no fabric to cut; the golden scissors
suspended in the mirror alone
symbolize the power of belief over reality. As in many of the artist’s works
the mirror does not reflect the world but the story we tell ourselves — a reminder that delusion
when reinforced by ceremony and repetition
can pass for truth.
images.teaser: How much illusion do we tailor before we can no longer face ourselves?
images.filename: The Haute Couture Tailors of Delusions.jpg

images.name: Eureka! The Soap (Opera)
images.description: n Eureka! The Soap (Opera), the artist portrays himself mid-bath in a theatrical moment of revelation, triumphantly raising a soap bar inscribed with “Eureka!” while surrounded by classical props, warm light, and playful absurdity. A foam crown adorns his head like a philosopher-king, a rubber duck floats serenely nearby, and a Greek flag subtly anchors the scene in historical reference. The composition combines humor, symbolism, and compositional precision to stage a modern reenactment of Archimedes’ legendary discovery—with a distinctly personal twist.
themes: This image playfully explores the boundary between intellect and absurdity
drawing on historical mythology
scientific epiphany

and domestic banality. The bath becomes a metaphorical space of solitude and inspiration—echoing the original “Eureka” legend while subverting it through self-aware theatricality. The work touches on the artist’s recurring themes of identity
transformation
and the re-enchantment of the everyday
all while reminding us that great ideas can surface in the most unexpected places—sometimes accompanied by soap and a rubber duck.
images.teaser: Why do revelations feel profound even when born from the most absurd moments?

images.name: I Cast a Giant Cats Shadow
images.description: n a dimly lit, box-like room, a surreal scene unfolds: one version of the artist lies motionless on a bed, grayscale and statue-like, while another, vibrant and lively, gleefully dances upon him. Behind them, a stern portrait of Erwin Schrödinger oversees the tableau, and the wall casts an unmistakable feline shadow—towering and unmistakably cat-shaped. A glowing sine wave arcs between the two figures, bridging life and potentiality, motion and stillness, in a space that feels both scientific and theatrical.
themes: "I Cast a Giant Cat's Shadow" is a witty and layered homage to Schrödinger’s famous quantum thought experiment. By inserting himself both as the living observer and the possibly-dead subject
the artist literalizes the paradox of being simultaneously alive and dead until observed. The playful pose of the dancing figure adds absurdity to the existential tension
while the giant cat's shadow
looming over the entire scene
serves as the omnipresent metaphor of quantum uncertainty. The work blends physics
mortality
and self-perception into a clever visual paradox
inviting the viewer to question not just reality—but their role in determining it.
images.teaser: What hidden life stirs within us when uncertainty becomes our natural state?
images.filename: I Cast a Giant Cats Shadow.jpg

images.name: Ink is Stronger Than Steel
images.description: Ink is Stronger Than Steel presents a striking and surreal composition in which two figures—both self-portraits of the artist—embody opposing forces in a dramatic duel. One figure, dressed as a musketeer, advances boldly, wielding a giant pen like a sword. The other, also a musketeer, turns to flee, visibly defeated, his steel sword slipping from his grasp and falling to the ground. A distant castle looms in the background, setting the stage for this symbolic confrontation, while cinematic lighting casts sharp contrasts that heighten the tension.
themes: This image reimagines the timeless adage “The pen is mightier than the sword” as an internal and philosophical struggle. By showing himself both as the victor with the pen and the vanquished who drops his sword
the artist stages a powerful allegory of the triumph of intellect
art
and expression over violence and brute force. The abandoned sword is not just a sign of retreat
but a metaphor for the relinquishment of aggression in favor of creative strength. The surreal
theatrical atmosphere underscores the universality of this theme
inviting reflection on the enduring supremacy of ideas over weapons.
images.teaser: Which force within us triumphs when thought dares confront brute aggression?

images.name: King Midas Nightmare
images.description: Bathed in warm bathroom light, a crowned elderly man—clad in a crimson polo and matching shorts, a dark towel-cape draped over his shoulders—sits on a toilet frozen in mid-panic. His mouth is stretched in a horrified scream, glasses glinting, one hand flung upward while the other clutches a length of toilet tissue that has begun an impossible metamorphosis: the pristine white sheet fades to gleaming gold toward the far end. The roll still mounted beside him hints that the transmutation is spreading, turning an everyday necessity into a useless treasure and trapping its royal victim in a private crisis.
themes: King Midas’s Nightmare translates the ancient cautionary tale of insatiable greed into a wry domestic tableau
underscoring how the coveted “golden touch” becomes a curse when it intrudes upon life’s most basic moments. By staging the myth in a mundane restroom
the image juxtaposes opulence with vulnerability
exposing the absurdity of wealth that nullifies function and comfort. The protagonist’s advanced age and self-mocking regalia add layers of self-portrait humor and existential reflection
suggesting that even a lifetime of achievements (or wishes) can culminate in unexpected
self-inflicted frustrations. Ultimately
the work satirizes humanity’s persistent hunger for more
reminding viewers that unchecked desire can turn necessities into burdens—and triumph into terror—when reality refuses to accommodate fantasy.
images.teaser: What do we fear becoming when desire turns even relief into humiliation?

images.name: No Signal Down the Rabbit's Hole
images.description: In this dynamic, surreal self-portrait, a man is seen tumbling downward through a glowing spiral tunnel, his limbs flailing and face contorted between wonder and distress. His hat and smartphone float beside him, the latter displaying a chilling message: "No Signal." The tunnel’s textured, bluish-green walls evoke a rabbit hole, both organic and uncanny, drawing the viewer into a disorienting descent. The sharp lighting and tilted perspective heighten the sensation of vertigo and isolation, emphasizing the surreal quality of this fall into the unknown.
themes: “No Sinal Down the Rabbit’s Hole” vividly explores the themes of disconnection
disorientation
and existential surrender. Referencing Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland
the image modernizes the plunge into the subconscious by replacing fantasy with the anxiety of technological isolation—embodied in the "No Signal" phone. The loss of control
symbolized by the floating hat and the spiraling void

suggests a fall not only into an unknown world but also into the self—where certainty and connection dissolve. The image can be read as a visual metaphor for confronting the surreal absurdities of the modern human condition
where identity
communication
and direction are all in flux.
images.teaser: What part of us keeps falling when certainty loses all connection?
images.filename: No Signal Down the Rabbit's Hole.jpg

images.name: Modern Econony_Exploit and Dispose
images.description: A seemingly placid kitchen tableau features a tall glass of fresh orange juice flanked by whole citrus, a plastic juicer, and several “halved fruits.” On second look those halves are miniaturised slices of your own head: skin-toned, ear-bearing, and neatly bisected so that the crown becomes a stand-in for orange rind while pulpy yellow flesh fills the interior. Warm diffuse light bathes the beige countertop, and a giant maple-leaf silhouette projected on the wall behind casts branching shadows that criss-cross the scene, hinting at nature now reduced to décor.
themes: The image weaves a biting allegory of consumer capitalism in which you
the artist
become both commodity and casualty: miniature slices of your own head replace orange halves

showing how modern economies “juice” individual identity and labor for maximum yield before discarding the exhausted remains. The reassuring glow of a domestic kitchen and a decorative maple-leaf silhouette mask the underlying brutality
exposing how systemic exploitation is prettified and normalized in everyday rituals of consumption. By literalizing self-sacrifice while relegating nature to a flattened backdrop
the work critiques the way both human beings and the environment are objectified
consumed
and ultimately treated as disposable within profit-driven cycles.
images.teaser: How much of ourselves can be consumed before nothing human remains unjuiced?

images.name: Paradox Stairways at Sundown
images.description: Under a burnished sunset sky, a sprawling desert hosts multiple ivory Penrose staircases, their impossible loops rising like silent monuments. Instead of climbing, every iteration of the artist now bolts away: one in the foreground sprinting toward the camera, others peeling off in divergent directions, their shadows stretching across cracked earth while faint dust plumes mark their urgency. The once-inviting steps stand vacant, glowing warmly in the last light, as distant duplicates vanish toward the horizon, leaving only spirals of stone against the vast expanse.
themes: The sudden exodus reframes the work from a meditation on relentless ambition to a dramatic rejection of cyclical striving—an act of self-liberation from the trap of “endless ascent.” By multiplying himself yet synchronising all figures in flight
the artist underscores the unity of instinct across divergent selves: when the paradox becomes clear
the only rational move is departure. The vacant staircases evoke abandoned ideologies or goals
while the setting sun amplifies themes of finite time and the urgency of choosing new paths. Altogether
the piece interrogates ambition
autonomy
and the courage required to step off a loop—even one of our own making.
images.teaser: What makes us flee the very ambitions that once gave us purpose?

images.name: In the Princes Training Cellar for Aspiring Dictators
images.description: n a vaulted stone cellar lit by a single torch, a man in a modern suit and tie teeters across a narrow plank bridging two barrels. On one side rests a golden crown, gleaming with the promise of rule; on the other, venomous cobras coil and rise in warning, guarded by a calm, watchful Niccolò Machiavelli holding a set of scales. The atmosphere is tense, symbolic, and deliberate, every element placed to distill a lesson in power.
themes: This image visualizes a maxim from Chapter 15 of Machiavelli’s The Prince

where the ruler must avoid personal vices that could lead to infamy and the loss of power. The precarious plank embodies the delicate balance between ambition and ruin; one misstep leads either to the crown or to deadly consequences. Machiavelli’s presence
scales in hand
represents the cold
pragmatic calculation necessary for survival in politics. The cobras personify the dangers of moral failings and political misjudgments
while the crown tempts with glory. Set in a “training cellar
” the scene becomes an initiation trial for novice rulers—an allegory of the constant need to measure every action against its potential cost to authority and survival.
images.teaser: What do we risk becoming when power demands balance above conscience?
images.filename: In the Princes Training Cellar for Aspiring Dictators.jpg

images.name: The Endless Stairway
images.description: The image presents a surreal urban scene where the same elderly man appears multiple times on an infinite looping staircase set within a modern architectural courtyard. The spiral staircase twists and curves upon itself, offering no true ascent or descent, but rather an endless circuit of movement. The warm evening light casts a contemplative mood, while the man’s repetitions suggest a fragmented journey, as if he is simultaneously at different points in time along the same path.
themes: This image explores themes of futility
repetition
and the human condition within the modern world. The endless staircase becomes a metaphor for life’s cyclical struggles
where effort and movement do not necessarily lead to progress. The multiple iterations of the same figure evoke questions of identity
aging
and the passage of time—suggesting that one’s life can feel like walking in circles
bound to structures larger than oneself. The modern office buildings enclosing the staircase amplify a sense of existential entrapment
reflecting both the monotony of routine and the search for meaning within an unyielding system. Ultimately
the image resonates as a visual allegory of persistence within limitation.
images.teaser: Why do we keep climbing when repetition has already replaced progress?

images.name: The Hand of Time
images.description: A lone man stands barefoot on cracked desert ground, his arms raised as if in invocation, encircled by monumental Roman numerals arranged like a broken sundial. The setting sun casts long shadows across the barren landscape, its golden light staining the scene with an air of desolation and timelessness. His figure, dressed in dark clothing and crowned with a hat, faces the fading horizon as though measuring himself against eternity.
themes: This surreal composition embodies the tension between human existence and the inexorable passage of time. The man
suspended in a ritual-like gesture
seems both master and captive of the monumental clock encircling him—a symbol of humanity’s attempt to measure
control
and understand time
even as it slips away. The desert evokes emptiness and impermanence
reinforcing the fragility of life against the cosmic scale of eternity. Shadows stretching toward the horizon suggest the fleeting nature of presence
while the broken sundial alludes to the futility of measuring time in a world where meaning dissolves under the weight of mortality. Ultimately
the work reflects on the paradox of human striving: our desire to transcend time while remaining bound by its unyielding march.
images.teaser: What gesture of defiance survives when time surrounds us on every side?

images.name: Unzipped Horizon
images.description: A lone man, viewed from behind, stands barefoot on wet sand at sunset. He points toward the glowing sun sinking into the horizon. Across the sea stretches an improbable zipper-like chain, gleaming metallic against the fiery sky, as though unfastening the ocean itself. The scene is bathed in deep orange light with serene reflections.
themes: Revelation and control over cosmic boundaries: the zipper proposes unsealing reality to expose hidden truth. The human figure—small yet assertive—gestures toward time’s edge
blending playfulness with metaphysical yearning. Motifs: mortality
time’s threshold
surreal engineering of nature
wonder vs. insignificance.
images.teaser: What hidden truth do we long to unzip at the edge of existence?

images.name: The Pacemaker Whisper
images.description: An elderly man stands against a stark black background, his expression contemplative and tinged with solemnity. His body reveals a glowing golden skeleton superimposed upon his real form, as though the viewer can see beneath the skin. Within his ribcage shines a radiant heart-shaped light, suggesting both a biological pacemaker and the symbolic essence of life. In his hand, extended outward, he holds a golden chain suspending a second luminous heart, glowing like a pendulum. The warm yellows of the skeleton and hearts contrast with the surrounding darkness, emphasizing fragility, mortality, and the rhythm of existence.
themes: The image explores the delicate balance between mortality
technology
and inner vitality. The glowing heart within the chest embodies the biological pacemaker — a device that sustains life — but also becomes a metaphor for memory
emotion
and the inner spark of being. The heart-shaped pendant
swinging like a pendulum
evokes the steady oscillations of time
rhythm
and inevitability
hinting at the fragility of human life as it ticks forward. The duality of internal versus external heart forms reflects the tension between mechanical survival and emotional essence. Symbolically
the work poses questions about the boundaries between biology
technology
and soul
inviting contemplation of aging
mortality
and the whispering pace that guides existence.
images.teaser: What keeps beating within us when the body depends on borrowed rhythm?

images.name: The Goldfish
images.description: An elderly man sits absorbed in reading a book, while a giant goldfish looms behind him. A surreal twist reveals another goldfish swimming inside his transparent head, as if his thoughts are alive and echoing the story in his hands.
themes: The image resonates deeply with Etgar Keret’s short story
in which a magical goldfish grants wishes but at the cost of personal and ethical dilemmas. By placing a goldfish inside the protagonist’s mind
the artwork suggests that these dilemmas are not external fables but inner struggles—questions of morality
memory

and desire living within us. The enormous goldfish in the background symbolizes the overwhelming presence of unspoken wishes and the haunting weight of “what if.” The act of reading merges the man’s identity with Keret’s narrative
suggesting that literature does not merely tell stories but colonizes the mind
shaping how we imagine choices
regrets
and responsibilities. Thus
the image touches on memory
morality
imagination
and the fragility of wishes
inviting viewers to confront the paradox between the freedom to desire and the ethical cost of fulfilling those desires.
images.teaser: What desires swim inside us when imagination and conscience share one mind?

images.name: Down into the Subconscious
images.description: Profile self‑portrait of an elderly man wearing glasses, lit from the left, with a cutaway cranium revealing a cavernous, keyhole‑shaped tunnel.
