Inside, a cloaked figure ascends a long staircase toward a warm light; the palette is muted teal and ochre with soft shadow cast on the wall.
themes: Introspection and the architecture of the mind; the staircase as effort
growth
and memory retrieval; the cave/tunnel as the subconscious; the lone inner figure as the ‘self within self’; aging and the search for meaning; thresholds
liminality
and enlightenment through persistence.
images.teaser: What must we descend through before light begins to resemble understanding?

images.name: The Never-Forgetting Mirror
images.description: An elderly man dressed in black stands pensively before a large ornate mirror in a dimly lit green room. Within the mirror are layered photographs of his younger self — from childhood through youth — alongside a smiling woman, perhaps his mother, and other figures from his past. The green light unifies the scene in a nostalgic haze, evoking both memory and timeless reflection.
themes: Memory
aging
and self-reflection. The image explores the persistence of the past within the present self
where the mirror acts as a portal to personal history. The monochromatic green palette conveys melancholy and introspection
suggesting that identity is an accumulation of remembered selves rather than a fixed state.
images.teaser: What part of us refuses forgetting when memory becomes a lifelong mirror?

images.name: Awaiting Nothing
images.description: An elderly man in a dark suit and hat stands on a fog-soaked, deserted street, eyes lowered to his wristwatch. All around him rise freestanding street clocks and a distant clock tower; none of them have hands—and his wristwatch is handless too. The wet cobblestones glimmer faintly under diffuse, bluish light. Buildings dissolve into mist, and the entire palette is subdued—steel blues and charcoal—suspending the scene between night and day, action and stasis.
themes: Time without measure
existence without direction. The absent hands erase chronology itself: there is no 'before' or 'after

' only duration without markers. Waiting becomes an ontological condition rather than an activity—a Beckettian pause where progress is impossible because measurement is impossible. The man’s private timepiece mirrors the public clocks: personal time and collective time have both failed
suggesting aging as an experience of de-synchronized reality. The fog reads as memory’s attrition; the slick stones imply erosion and recurrence. Within the broader corpus

'Awaiting' intensifies the Fading Horizons motif—time as a vanishing horizon line—transforming a city square into a metaphysical stage where expectation persists even when time itself has gone missing.
images.teaser: Who are we while waiting when time itself has stopped giving direction?

images.name: The Human Ostrich
images.description: Set in an arid, sun-bleached desert, a man kneels on the sand with his head literally buried in the ground. Around him, a semicircle of glowing computer and television screens flicker with images of world leaders and politicians—figures gesturing, shouting, or performing for the camera. Each screen casts a harsh, isolated light on the barren landscape, their electronic glow replacing the absent sun. A small ostrich appears far in the distance, mirroring the man’s posture like a ghostly double. The man’s body, pale and vulnerable against the vast emptiness, forms the only organic presence amid the sterile machinery of mediated reality.
themes: Denial
complicity

and the paralysis of perception. The image is a mordant allegory of humanity’s tendency to escape truth by immersion in distraction. The desert—void of life and nourishment—symbolizes a moral and emotional wasteland
while the electronic screens function as modern idols
broadcasting noise that replaces genuine awareness. The man’s self-imposed blindness literalizes the idiom 'burying one’s head in the sand
' transforming it into a tragic act of surrender. Psychologically

the work exposes a fractured consciousness overwhelmed by the spectacle of power and manipulation. The ostrich in the distance becomes a mocking echo—a natural creature imitated by a human who has unlearned the instinct to survive. Politically
it indicts collective passivity in the face of corruption and demagoguery; philosophically
it continues Fuchs’s exploration of self-erasure within the digital age
where information abundance leads not to enlightenment but to despair. Within the broader corpus
'The Human Ostrich' stands as a visual manifesto of Fading Horizons—a confrontation with the emptiness that follows when humanity trades introspection for spectacle.
images.teaser: What truth do we bury when denial feels safer than seeing clearly?

images.name: The Persistence of Old Habits
images.description: Three versions of the same man stand on a rain‑soaked New York street, each holding a bare umbrella frame. The central figure faces the viewer, the left figure signals for a cab, and the right figure stands with his back turned, paused mid‑stride. Fog softens taxis and tall buildings, creating a muted, surreal atmosphere. The fabric‑less umbrellas expose all three figures to the rain, emphasizing the quiet tension between outward poise and the futility of their repeated gesture.
themes: The work examines how habits persist long after their purpose fades. The three figures represent parallel inner states—confrontation
action

and withdrawal— coexisting within one psyche. Their repetition in a fog‑filled urban setting reflects the monotony of modern routines and the isolation beneath polished appearances. The umbrella frames symbolize empty rituals performed out of instinct rather than need
highlighting vulnerability masked by formality. This creates a meditation on identity
denial
and the unconscious pull of patterns that endure despite their obsolescence.
images.teaser: Why do useless habits survive long after meaning and shelter have vanished?

images.name: The Gap
images.description: Two iterations of you stand at the severed ends of a collapsed bridge, suspended above still water under a muted, fog-filled sky. One figure holds his head in frustration while the other observes the void calmly, hands in pockets. A One Way sign stands ironically beside the broken edge, offering direction where no passage exists. The atmosphere is hushed, cold, and motionless, emphasizing the impossibility of crossing and the tension between the two isolated selves.
themes: The image evokes the inner dialogue between conflicting states of mind—confusion versus contemplation—set against an irrevocable rupture. The broken bridge symbolizes paths that can no longer be followed
decisions that divide one’s identity

and distances time has carved between past and present selves. The One Way sign becomes a symbol of irony: life’s insistence on forward motion despite circumstances that prevent it. Themes of isolation
existential hesitation
and the quiet struggle of aging are embedded in the scene
forming a psychological landscape shaped by uncertainty and introspection.
images.teaser: What remains possible when the bridge between our two selves has collapsed?

images.name: The Eternal Kiss
images.description: A solitary man stands before an immense abstract sculpture glowing in the last blaze of a descending sun. Within the sculpture’s fluid, intertwined forms, two figures appear locked in an eternal embrace, their bodies curving toward one another in a suspended kiss. The material seems almost translucent, illuminated from within as though the emotion it contains radiates outward. The reflective ground stretches the scene into infinity, while the vast sky burns with the final intensity of daylight. The encounter between man, monument, and fading sun creates a moment of hushed reverence, as if he is witnessing love preserved beyond the reach of time.
themes: The work explores the persistence of intimacy long after its living moment has passed. The two figures
forever kissing within the sculpture’s embracing forms
symbolize love held in stasis—untouched by decay yet unreachable to the solitary observer. Their preserved kiss becomes an ideal
a memory
or a longing
depending on the viewer’s inner landscape. The sunset serves as a reminder of transience
casting the encounter between past affection and present solitude into a poignant equilibrium. It is a meditation on what endures
what fades
and the emotional distances that remain even when love appears monumental.
images.teaser: Can love endure when intimacy survives only as an untouchable form?

images.name: Farewell to Gravity
images.description: A man rushes forward, reaching desperately toward his fleeing hat as it is carried by unseen forces through a labyrinth of colossal hovering structures. The architecture feels ancient yet futuristic, solid yet impossibly weightless. A golden mist saturates the air, casting every surface in ambiguous light, blurring the boundary between dream and physical reality. The man’s outstretched arm conveys urgency, while the quiet expanse beneath him reinforces his vulnerability within a world slipping out of balance.
themes: The composition reflects humanity’s timeless struggle against forces beyond its control
both external and internal. Gravity becomes a metaphor for stability
identity
and the systems that normally anchor us. The fleeing hat represents the fragile symbols through which we define ourselves—status
self-image
continuity. The floating structures hint at existential uncertainty
evoking a world where the rules no longer align with experience. The result is a surreal allegory of disorientation
resilience
and the absurd persistence of hope.
images.teaser: What do we become when the forces grounding us suddenly let go?

images.name: The Headless Majority
images.description: Headless men walk a rain-soaked urban street, their posture and clothing immaculate, their hands absorbed in the glow of their smartphones. Their missing heads transform them into anonymous silhouettes, while the wet pavement and muted morning light give the street an uncomfortable stillness. A single man with an intact head moves among them, his presence both reassuring and isolating. The emptiness above the others’ collars creates an uncanny tension, suggesting a world where identity has quietly evaporated.
themes: The work examines the erosion of individuality in the age of digital distraction. The headless figures symbolize the surrender of agency and thought
their attention consumed by devices that replace awareness. The lone headed figure embodies the remaining spark of consciousness
caught between belonging and alienation. The rainy street underscores the emotional numbness of modern routine
while the image’s uncanny atmosphere invites reflection on presence
absence
and the cost of disconnecting from one’s own mind.
images.teaser: How much selfhood survives when distraction quietly replaces thought and identity?

images.name: This is not an email
images.description: The image depicts a surreal encounter at the threshold of a doorway. On the left, a uniformed postal worker, identifiable by the USPS insignia and leather mailbag, extends an envelope toward a man standing inside the doorway. On the right, the recipient wears a dark suit, white shirt, and blue tie, topped with a fedora, his body partially illuminated by warm interior light. Both figures are conspicuously headless, their necks abruptly cut off, leaving hats and clothing to suggest identity in the absence of faces. The envelope, held at the center of the composition, bears the clearly legible text: “This is NOT an e-mail.” The doorframe acts as a visual and symbolic boundary between exterior and interior, public duty and private space, while the lighting reinforces this division—cooler tones outside, warmer tones within.
themes: The image operates as a metaphor for communication in an era of abstraction and disembodiment. By removing the heads
the artist eliminates individuality
cognition
and emotion
reducing both sender and recipient to functional roles: messenger and receiver. The envelope
emphatically asserting its physicality in contrast to digital communication
becomes a relic of tangible human exchange
yet it is delivered to someone who is literally missing the seat of awareness. This tension suggests a critique of modern communication: messages persist
systems function
and transactions occur
but meaning
presence
and personal engagement are eroded. The doorway underscores a liminal state—between old and new
material and virtual
human contact and automated process—while the headless figures imply that identity itself has become secondary to systems
uniforms
and protocols. The work thus reflects on anonymity
bureaucracy
and the quiet absurdity of connection in a world where communication survives even as the human core seems to vanish.
images.teaser: What remains of communication when no true self stands behind the message?

images.name: Head in the Clouds
images.description: A meticulously constructed surreal tableau in which an elderly man, dressed in a black coat and hat, stands before an easel set against a monumental sky of sculpted clouds. On the easel rests a headless suit—a fully realized body without identity—its matching hat floating in calm suspension above the empty collar. The protagonist, seen from behind, lifts a paintbrush toward this absent self, creating an ambiguous moment between making, revealing, and confronting. The vast cloudscape functions not merely as a backdrop but as a symbolic extension of the psyche—soft, shifting, immeasurable. The highly controlled lighting, poised posture, and silent impossibility of the floating hat form a delicate balance between photographic realism and metaphysical estrangement. The scene presents a quiet ritual of self-construction, self-erasure, and self-inquiry, suspended outside of time.
themes: A profound meditation on identity
absence

and authorship. The headless suit represents the hollowed or fragmented self—an external shell awaiting definition—while the floating hat hints at a consciousness dislocated from its physical form. The painter-protagonist embodies both creator and subject

suggesting a recursive exploration of the self as object and observer. The act of painting becomes a metaphor for constructing personal meaning amid internal ambiguity. The luminous clouds evoke states of dreaming
introspection
and the unknowable layers of the mind. The image engages with themes of self-perception
existential detachment
the fragile boundary between presence and void
and the paradox of shaping an identity that simultaneously eludes completion. It situates the figure within a psychological landscape where creation and disappearance coexist
raising questions about authorship
autonomy
and the limits of self-knowledge.
images.teaser: Who paints us into being when the self arrives already hollowed out?

images.name: The Lying Camera
images.description: The image presents a domestic living room scene captured through a layered visual construction. In the foreground, the back of a digital camera dominates the lower portion of the frame, its screen displaying a live view of the same room. Within this screen, a man lies sprawled on the floor, arms extended, as if fallen or suspended in mid-collapse. In the background—outside the camera screen—the same man appears again, now standing on the couch with raised arms, caught in a moment of intense physical reaction. A television on the right shows a soccer match frozen at a dramatic instant near the goal, while framed artworks hang askew on the wall, echoing a sense of imbalance. The room itself is tidy and familiar: sofas, cushions, bookshelves, decorative objects—elements of everyday domestic life—yet subtly destabilized by the skewed angles, duplicated figure, and the recursive presence of the camera observing its own scene.
themes: The image explores the fragmentation of experience in an age of mediated reality
where life is simultaneously lived and recorded
reacted to and archived. The doubled figure suggests a split between physical presence and self-observation: one self is immersed in the emotional immediacy of the televised event
while the other exists only as an image
flattened and immobilized within the camera’s frame. The soccer match functions as a catalyst rather than a subject
symbolizing collective spectacle and emotional outsourcing—moments that provoke intense reaction yet remain fundamentally external and mediated. By placing the camera prominently in the composition
the work foregrounds the act of documentation itself
questioning whether experience is fully real unless it is captured. The tilted artworks and the contrast between action and collapse reinforce a sense of instability
suggesting that contemporary identity is negotiated across multiple layers of attention: screen
memory
representation
and lived body. The image ultimately reflects on how the act of watching—and recording oneself watching—can fracture presence
turning even private moments into performative
self-referential constructs.
images.teaser: What part of reality do we lose when life is lived through devices?

images.name: The Cloud Dragger
images.description: An elderly man, formally dressed in a dark suit and hat, stands on a vast, cracked desert floor under an open sky. He leans forward with visible strain, gripping a rope that extends behind him and is improbably attached to a large, voluminous cloud. The cloud, dense yet luminous, drags along the ground as though it were a solid mass rather than vapor, its soft contours contrasting sharply with the arid, fractured earth. The man’s posture conveys effort and resistance; his body is angled against the pull of the cloud, suggesting physical exertion and determination. The environment is sparse and unforgiving, with no other human presence or landmarks, emphasizing the solitary nature of the act.
themes: The image constructs a powerful visual paradox by transforming an intangible
weightless cloud into a burden that must be hauled across an inhospitable landscape. This inversion foregrounds themes of existential absurdity and the human compulsion to control
possess
or carry what is inherently uncontrollable. The act of dragging the cloud can be read as a metaphor for memory
regret
ambition
or meaning itself—elements born in the realm of the abstract that nevertheless weigh heavily on lived experience. The figure’s age intensifies the reading: persistence becomes both heroic and tragic
a refusal to relinquish purpose even when effort outweighs outcome. The cracked earth underscores the cost of this struggle
suggesting depletion and erosion over time
while the cloud retains a deceptive beauty
hinting that the very things that burden us are also those that give life its poetic dimension.
images.teaser: Why do we drag burdens that should vanish the moment we touch them?

images.name: The Vacuum Cleaner
images.description: An elderly man is caught mid-motion on a tiled floor, his body partially lifted and dragged backward as a massive vacuum cleaner engulfs him. His torso is already swallowed by the machine, while his outstretched arms claw forward in a last, desperate attempt to hold onto scattered documents and photographs—records of identity, memory, and personal history. Papers lie chaotically beneath him, some already slipping beyond reach, as the vacuum’s dark mechanical body dominates the frame. The scene captures the precise moment of irreversible absorption: a human life, its memories and proof of existence, being consumed whole.
themes: This image is not about threat or approach; it is about active erasure in progress. The vacuum cleaner is a devouring force—systemic
indifferent
and unstoppable—consuming not only physical objects but identity itself. It represents the mechanisms that absorb individuals into anonymity: bureaucracy
digital oblivion
institutional power
aging

and ultimately mortality. The man’s futile resistance underscores the psychological violence of this process: memory and selfhood are not gently lost but mechanically erased. The work confronts the viewer with an existential truth—no matter how fiercely one clings to personal history
there are forces that will swallow both the person and the evidence that they were ever here. The image asks whether identity has intrinsic value once it becomes inconvenient to preserve
images.teaser: What is left of us when erasure is already happening in plain sight?

images.name: Internal Clock_3
images.description: The image presents a frontal, tightly framed portrait of an elderly, bald man wearing glasses, his upper body bare and rendered with unflinching realism. At the center of his chest, where the heart would normally reside, a large antique mechanical clock is embedded into his torso, its brass casing and exposed gears merging seamlessly with flesh. The clock face, marked with Roman numerals, is intact and dominant, while the surrounding skin appears torn open, suggesting an invasive yet strangely natural integration of mechanism and body. The man’s expression is calm but grave, almost resigned, his gaze directed straight at the viewer. Behind him, a softly blurred background echoes the circular forms and golden tones of clockwork, reinforcing the sense that time surrounds and permeates him entirely. The lighting is controlled and sculptural, emphasizing texture—aged skin, polished metal, and the fine details of the clock’s inner workings
themes: The image functions as a powerful meditation on time
mortality
and identity in old age. By replacing the heart with a clock
the work literalizes the idea that time has become the governing force of existence
no longer an abstract concept but a physical
internal presence. The mechanical heart suggests both endurance and inevitability: the body may weaken
but time continues its relentless motion. There is a tension between humanity and mechanism—flesh versus brass
vulnerability versus precision—raising questions about whether a person is sustained by memory
by biology
or by the accumulated ticking of lived moments. The subject’s direct
unsentimental gaze resists melodrama
implying acceptance rather than fear. In this sense
the image is less about death than about awareness: a conscious reckoning with the finite nature of life
where time is no longer something one measures externally
but something one quite literally carries within.
images.teaser: Who are we when time begins beating where the heart once governed?

images.name: Time on Thin Ice
images.description: A surreal scene shows two identical elderly men dressed in dark coats and hats, separated by a large glass window. One stands safely inside a minimalist bedroom, while the other balances on a small iceberg floating in a calm, endless sea, holding an umbrella. The interior and exterior merge seamlessly, emphasizing the thin boundary between comfort and exposure.
themes: The image explores self-division
mortality
and the illusion of control in the face of inevitable forces. The doubled figure represents the split between awareness and action: one self observes
protected and detached
while the other endures direct vulnerability. The iceberg and umbrella function as metaphors for the fragility of human defenses against time
aging
and environmental or existential collapse
positioning the work as a quiet yet incisive reflection on denial
responsibility
and the psychology of witnessing one’s own precarity.
images.teaser: What illusion protects us when another self is already standing on thin ice?

images.name: The Inner Stalker
images.description: In a stark, concrete interior defined by heavy pillars and narrow stairways, two identical elderly men occupy the same space at different moments of intention. One figure ascends a staircase bathed in warm, amber light, his body angled forward as he disappears toward an unseen destination. The other remains behind, partially concealed by a massive concrete column, his back turned to the viewer, one hand pressed firmly against the surface as if for balance or reassurance. His head tilts just enough to observe the climber, suggesting vigilance without intervention. The architecture dominates the scene, its cold, textured surfaces amplifying the contrast between motion and stillness, illumination and shadow, departure and pause.
themes: The image explores the divided self at the moment of decision
portraying an inner tension between action and hesitation. The ascending figure embodies resolve
transition
or acceptance of change
while the hidden observer represents self-surveillance
doubt
or the part of the psyche that resists closure. Rather than confrontation
the relationship between the two is marked by distance and restraint
emphasizing internal conflict as a quiet

ongoing process. The concrete column functions as a psychological barrier—both separating and supporting—while the contrasting light suggests that clarity is visible but not universally accessible. Ultimately
the work reflects on aging
agency
and self-awareness
presenting identity not as a unified whole but as a continuous negotiation between moving forward and watching oneself do so.
images.teaser: What part of us is following when we try to outrun ourselves?

images.name: In Between
images.description: In Between depicts two nearly identical elderly men meeting within the interior of a Möbius strip–like structure, a continuous surface that curves and twists without a discernible beginning or end. One figure, wearing a hat, appears to approach or guide; the other stands bareheaded, receptive. Their restrained handshake takes place in a dim, enveloping space where orientation is uncertain and scale is ambiguous. The sculptural surface around them feels both architectural and abstract, enclosing the encounter while dissolving conventional distinctions between inside and outside, before and after.
themes: The work explores liminality as a psychological and existential condition—being suspended between identities
moments
or states of self. The Möbius strip functions as a metaphor for continuity without resolution
suggesting that personal identity is not linear but recursive
folding back on itself in subtle
often imperceptible ways. The encounter between the two figures can be read as a confrontation between different versions of the same self—past and present
memory and awareness—without privileging either. Rather than offering reconciliation or transformation
In Between dwells in the tension of recognition
proposing that meaning resides not in arrival or departure
but in the fragile
unsettling space where certainty dissolves and self-understanding remains perpetually unfinished.
images.teaser: Who are we between selves when no clear edge separates one from another?

images.name: The Missing Part
images.description: A sculptural, monochrome portrait presents an elderly male face resembling a classical protoma, its surface fractured into interlocking puzzle-like segments. One central piece is missing, revealing a soft, depth-suggesting void rather than a solid interior. The remaining facial features—especially the eyes—retain their presence and calm, while the head emerges against a dark, cracked marble background, echoing fracture and endurance. The image balances solidity and absence, stillness and disruption, as if the face exists in a state of suspended incompleteness.
themes: The work explores identity not as something accumulated
but as something partially lost. The missing piece does not reveal what lies beneath; instead

it marks the limits of self-knowledge and the irreversibility of certain absences. Fragmentation here is not violent but structural—an accepted condition rather than a crisis. By refusing to fill the void with narrative or imagery
the image suggests that what is missing cannot be recovered or explained
only acknowledged. The portrait becomes a meditation on aging
memory
and the quiet coexistence between what remains and what can no longer be reassembled.
images.teaser: What missing piece keeps us searching for a self we cannot complete?

images.name: The Dilemma
images.description: In a dimly lit, sparsely furnished room where two walls converge into a narrow corner, a solitary bald man stands with his back to the viewer, one hand resting on his head in a gesture of hesitation. Before him, two ornate mirrors serve as portals rather than reflections. From the right mirror, a lion strides forward, its powerful body breaching the gilded frame, eyes fixed with sovereign authority. From the left, a fox steps out cautiously, alert and calculating, its presence defined by sharp intelligence rather than brute force. In the shadowed corner between the two mirrors stands a dark, almost spectral figure—recognizable as Machiavelli—watching silently, as though observing the unfolding experiment of his own political doctrine.
themes: The scene becomes an embodiment of Machiavelli’s famous assertion in The Prince that a ruler must learn to be both lion and fox—strong enough to frighten wolves and cunning enough to recognize traps. The animals
emerging from mirrors
suggest that these qualities are latent within the protagonist

not external forces imposed upon him. The mirrors function as psychological thresholds: what was once theoretical or reflective becomes embodied and unavoidable. Machiavelli’s shadowed presence transforms the room into a philosophical chamber

where theory confronts lived reality. The protagonist’s uncertain posture indicates the existential weight of choice—strength without cunning risks downfall; cunning without strength invites contempt. The work thus explores power not as a simple attribute but as a moral and psychological balancing act
questioning whether leadership is a matter of character
strategy
or performance.
images.teaser: Which self leads us when cunning and force demand opposite kinds of courage?

images.name: The Echo Chamber
images.description: In a vast, concave chamber clad in green metallic panels, the same elderly man appears multiple times along the curved walls, each figure caught mid-gesture with hands cupped around his mouth as if calling out. The architecture bends inward, forming a distorted spherical enclosure that warps perspective and compresses space. The central figure stands closest to the viewer, his posture tense and urgent, while the other iterations recede along the curvature, slightly blurred and diminished in scale. The repetition of the body, combined with the seamless metallic surface, creates the sensation of an echo chamber where sound, movement, and identity reverberate without clear origin or destination.
themes: The image explores the idea of the self as both echo and resistance within an enclosed psychological space. The concave metallic chamber functions as a metaphorical echo chamber—an inward-folding mind where every proclamation ultimately reverberates back to its source. The repeated upright figures suggest insistence
the need to be heard

or perhaps the compulsion to affirm one’s existence through repetition. Yet the bent figure complicates this reading. He introduces vulnerability into the system of echoes. If the standing figures embody assertion and projection
the bent one represents interruption—fatigue
doubt
or introspection. His posture suggests listening rather than shouting
withdrawal rather than declaration. By breaking the uniformity
he transforms the image from a simple metaphor of amplification into a layered meditation on internal conflict: the tension between speaking and silencing
between the urge to project identity outward and the impulse to fold inward. The chamber thus becomes not merely an acoustic space
but a psychological one—where the self debates itself across multiple positions of strength and fragility.
images.teaser: What grows inside us when our own voice returns louder than truth?

images.name: Modern Sisyphus
images.description: A suited elderly man strains forward, pushing an enormous, rough-hewn boulder up the sharp incline of a metallic arrow that points upward like a financial growth chart. The arrow zigzags steeply toward the sky, suspended against a muted, foggy urban backdrop. Atop the boulder sits a small monkey, calmly balanced and observing the ascent. The man’s posture is tense and determined, his body angled forward in effort, while the monkey remains light, almost indifferent, perched at the summit of the burden he propels.
themes: This image reimagines the myth of Sisyphus within a contemporary framework of progress and evolution. The upward-pointing arrow evokes economic growth
ambition
and the relentless demand for advancement
while the boulder symbolizes the enduring weight of struggle. The monkey introduces a provocative counterpoint: despite humanity’s aspiration toward progress—technological
financial
or intellectual—our primal nature remains seated atop our achievements. The tension between the striving human figure and the calm
evolutionary ancestor suggests that what we call “progress” may merely be the repetition of an ancient pattern
repackaged in modern form. The image questions whether ascent truly signifies transcendence
or whether we are simply pushing the same existential burden up ever-steeper slopes
accompanied by the inescapable remnants of our origins.
images.teaser: How long do we keep climbing when effort itself has become our punishment?

images.name: Your Time Is Up
images.description: Three elevator doors stand open in a polished, modern interior overlooking a vast coastal landscape at sunset. In each doorway appears the same elderly man in different attire and orientation. On the left, he steps forward in a dark suit and hat, holding a closed umbrella, facing the viewer as if arriving or departing with purpose. In the center, a casually dressed version of the man stands with his back to us, hands on hips, confronting a large antique clock positioned where the elevator interior should be. On the right, another suited and hatted version walks away toward the glowing horizon. Above each elevator door, illuminated indicators display arrows and the number “2,” suggesting movement upward or downward. The warm golden light of the sunset reflects on the glossy floor, creating a quiet yet charged atmosphere.
themes: This image stages a confrontation with time
choice
and existential direction. The elevators—symbols of vertical movement and transition—become metaphors for life’s possible trajectories: ascent
descent
arrival
departure. The central clock interrupts the expected functionality of the elevator
transforming a space of movement into a space of reckoning. Time replaces destination. The three figures represent temporal states of the self—past
present
and future—coexisting within the same architectural frame. One approaches
one contemplates
one retreats. The illuminated arrows and the repeated number “2” subtly evoke duality: up/down
forward/back
act/wait. The sunset beyond the doors reinforces the theme of finitude
suggesting that every direction is ultimately bounded by time’s horizon. Rather than offering a clear narrative
the image presents a suspended decision: when confronted with the knowledge that “your time is up
” do you step forward
turn back
or stand still and face the clock?
images.teaser: What choice remains when time turns every open door into a verdict?

images.name: Jarred
images.description: A wooden shelf in a dimly lit space holds a row of large glass jars sealed with red lids. Inside each jar appears the same elderly man, compressed within the transparent container as if preserved like a specimen. Although the figure is identical in every jar, his posture changes from one container to another: in one he raises a clenched fist, in another he covers his head in distress, elsewhere he sits quietly with folded hands, presses his palms against the glass as if trying to escape, or slumps forward in exhaustion. The jars are arranged methodically, like objects in a strange collection, and their dusty surfaces and muted lighting reinforce the sense that these captured moments have been stored for a long time.
themes: The image reads like a cabinet of psychological specimens
where different emotional states of the same individual—anger
despair
reflection
protest
and surrender—are isolated and displayed. The transparent glass creates a paradox: the man is fully visible
yet completely confined. This suggests that the prisons we inhabit are often internal rather than physical
formed by memory
fear
or self-perception. By multiplying the same person across several jars
the work transforms personal emotion into a symbolic archive of the human psyche
implying that the many versions of ourselves created by experience and time can coexist simultaneously. The shelf becomes a metaphorical storage space for identity
where fragments of the self are preserved like bottled moments of an inner life.
images.teaser: How many selves do we imprison before we recognize the cage is inward?
